text:
GU MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- ATTENDING PHYSICIAN NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review / Post-Olaparib Progression Assessment

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Mr. Gerald Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for evaluation following discontinuation of olaparib (Lynparza) due to disease progression with visceral metastases and cumulative transfusion-dependent anemia. He presents today for post-tumor board discussion, next-line therapy planning, and goals-of-care conversation.

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old retired civil engineer with a background of high-risk locally advanced prostate adenocarcinoma, initially diagnosed at age 67 with bulky bilateral disease, Gleason 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5), PSA 87.4 ng/mL, clinical stage cT3bN1M0 (AJCC Stage IVA). He was treated with curative intent using long-course ADT plus abiraterone (Zytiga)/prednisone combined with definitive IMRT to the prostate and pelvis (78 Gy to the prostate/seminal vesicles, 54 Gy to pelvic nodes). He achieved an excellent initial biochemical response with PSA nadir of 0.2 ng/mL.

Approximately 36 months into treatment, he developed biochemical castration-resistance -- PSA doubling time approximately 3.5 months, castrate testosterone confirmed. Germline testing (Myriad myRisk) revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22). Somatic tumor sequencing (Foundation One CDx on re-biopsy specimen) confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss with LOH, AR amplification (8x), TP53 p.R175H, RB1 deletion, and PTEN loss. MSI-stable, TMB low (4 mut/Mb). PSMA PET/CT at time of CRPC declaration identified low-volume metastatic disease: a right iliac chain node and early right posterior iliac crest bone lesion not visible on conventional imaging.

He was initiated on enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily with an initial PSA response (PSA 0.9 ng/mL at 3 months, nadir 0.7 ng/mL). However, rapid biochemical progression emerged by 9 months on enza (PSA doubling time ~6 weeks), and repeat bone scan showed new multifocal osseous metastases at T10, right 6th rib, and left iliac wing. MRI spine confirmed T10 metastasis without cord compression; he received palliative EBRT to T10 (30 Gy/10 fractions) with good pain response.

Given confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss, enzalutamide was discontinued and he was transitioned to olaparib 300 mg twice daily (per PROfound data), with concurrent continuation of leuprolide and addition of denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg monthly for bone protection. He had a notable initial PSA response (PSA nadir 0.6 ng/mL at 6 months on olaparib) and stable bone disease. However, routine restaging at 6 months identified a new 1.4 cm segment VII hepatic lesion, confirmed by CT-guided biopsy to be metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma -- PSA-positive, PSMA-positive, NKX3.1-positive -- without neuroendocrine transformation (synaptophysin, chromogranin, INSM1 all negative). Ki-67 was 35%, consistent with a highly proliferative tumor clone.

In parallel, olaparib caused progressive, treatment-limiting anemia. He required dose reduction to 200 mg BID at month 3 following grade 3 anemia (Hgb nadir 7.1 g/dL) requiring 2 units pRBC. Hematology was consulted; bone marrow biopsy showed hypocellular marrow with erythroid hypoplasia consistent with PARP inhibitor toxicity and no evidence of marrow infiltration. He has since required approximately 8 cumulative units of pRBC over the olaparib course, with Hgb falling to 7.5-8.0 g/dL between transfusions every 4-6 weeks. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) was initiated.

Most recently, PSA has risen to 1.8 ng/mL from nadir 0.6 ng/mL, meeting criteria for PSA progression with the hepatic biopsy-confirmed visceral metastasis. Olaparib was discontinued. He was referred to GU tumor board, and his case was formally presented and reviewed.

He reports worsening fatigue, decreased appetite with approximately 8 lbs weight loss over the past 2 months, and right upper quadrant discomfort. He denies new bone pain (T10 site well controlled since radiation), denies neurological symptoms, and denies dyspnea at rest. He remains ambulatory but has difficulty with prolonged activity.

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**
- Constitutional: fatigue (grade 2, limiting instrumental ADLs), decreased appetite, 8 lb unintentional weight loss over 2 months
- Neurological: no headache, no diplopia, no new focal weakness or sensory change, no bowel or bladder dysfunction
- Musculoskeletal: residual mid-back pain from T10 metastasis, well-controlled since radiation; no new bone pain
- Gastrointestinal: right upper quadrant discomfort, no jaundice, no nausea currently (prior nausea on olaparib now resolved), normal bowel habits
- Genitourinary: residual mild urinary frequency (post-RT change), no gross hematuria
- Cardiovascular: no dyspnea on exertion, no chest pain, no palpitations
- Hematologic: ongoing fatigue consistent with transfusion-dependent anemia; last transfusion approximately 2 weeks ago
- Endocrine: persistent hot flashes and night sweats on ADT

**PAST MEDICAL HISTORY**
- Prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 4+5=9, cT3bN1M0 at diagnosis) -- as detailed above
- Germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant (c.5946delT)
- Hypertension
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (managed with oral agents)
- Former smoker (20 pack-years, quit age 55)

**MEDICATIONS**
- Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (continued for castrate testosterone maintenance)
- Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SC every 4 weeks (bone metastasis management)
- Ferric carboxymaltose IV (per hematology protocol)
- Calcium 1000 mg orally daily / Vitamin D3 2000 IU daily (with denosumab)
- Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
- Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily (type 2 DM)
- Ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed (nausea; was used with olaparib, now on PRN basis)
- Olaparib 200 mg BID -- DISCONTINUED at this visit

**ALLERGIES**
No known drug allergies.

**SOCIAL HISTORY**
Retired civil engineer. Lives at home with wife, who is present at today's visit. Two adult children, both in good health. Former smoker (quit age 55). No alcohol or illicit drug use. Functional status: ECOG PS 2 (ambulatory, capable of self-care, unable to perform all usual activities). Drives himself to appointments. Has not required home health services to date.

**FAMILY HISTORY**
Father: prostate cancer diagnosed at age 65. Paternal uncle: deceased from prostate cancer. No known breast or ovarian cancer in family. No known colorectal cancer. (Germline BRCA2 cascade testing recommended for first-degree relatives; patient reports his two children were referred for genetic counseling.)

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**
- Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, weight 168 lbs (down from 176 lbs at last visit 2 months ago)
- General: Thin, pale-appearing elderly male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversant and engaged.
- HEENT: Pale conjunctivae, no scleral icterus, no lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains
- Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs; no peripheral edema
- Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally; no crackles or wheezes
- Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant, no rebound, no guarding. No hepatomegaly appreciated on palpation (note: hepatic lesion is segment VII by imaging). No ascites.
- Musculoskeletal: No midline spinal tenderness on percussion. Normal gait.
- Neurological: No focal deficits. Grossly intact cranial nerves. Strength 5/5 in all extremities.
- Skin: No rash, no petechiae, no purpura
- Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or supraclavicular adenopathy

**LABORATORY RESULTS**
- PSA: 1.8 ng/mL (rising from nadir 0.6 ng/mL; prior values 0.8, 1.2, 1.8 over last 3 monthly checks)
- Testosterone: 8 ng/dL (castrate)
- CBC: WBC 4.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 8.3 g/dL (post-transfusion day 14; grade 2 anemia), platelets 211,000/mcL
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: sodium 137, potassium 4.0, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m2), total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL, ALT 28 U/L, AST 32 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with osseous metastases)
- LDH: 248 U/L (mildly elevated)
- Albumin: 3.2 g/dL (mildly low, consistent with nutritional compromise)
- Ferritin: 124 ng/mL, iron saturation 24%

**IMAGING RESULTS**
Recent PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11): Multiple PSMA-avid bone metastases identified (T10, T12, L3, right posterior 6th rib, left iliac wing, right posterior iliac crest, new right femoral head -- 7 bone lesions total), PSMA-avid right iliac chain lymphadenopathy (1.3 cm, SUVmax 6.1), and PSMA-avid segment VII hepatic lesion (SUVmax 8.4, 1.8 cm). No brain lesions. High overall tumor PSMA expression (SUVmax range 3.8-8.4). No large-volume visceral disease. PSMA expression and distribution are consistent with eligibility criteria for lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (no dominant PSMA-negative lesion).

Prior MRI spine: T10 vertebral body metastasis without epidural extension or cord compression; L3 early marrow involvement. Post-radiation changes at T10.

**TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION SUMMARY**
Case presented at GU oncology multidisciplinary tumor board. Attendees included medical oncology, radiation oncology, interventional radiology, palliative care, and urologic oncology. Key clinical points discussed:

1. Disease status: mCRPC with bone (7 lesions), lymph node, and visceral (hepatic) metastases. Post-ARSI x2 (abiraterone then enzalutamide) and post-PARP inhibitor (olaparib). Taxane-naive.
2. Genomic profile: biallelic BRCA2 loss (germline + somatic LOH), AR amplification without ligand-binding domain mutations, TP53 mutant, RB1 loss, PTEN loss. No neuroendocrine transformation on hepatic biopsy. MSS, TMB low.
3. Functional status: ECOG PS 2 with significant anemia-related morbidity; preference to avoid chemotherapy expressed by patient.
4. PSMA eligibility: PSMA PET/CT demonstrates high PSMA avidity across all metastatic sites (hepatic SUVmax 8.4, bone lesions SUVmax 3.8-6.x). No dominant PSMA-negative lesion identified. Meets imaging eligibility criteria for 177Lu-PSMA-617.
5. Options considered: (a) cabazitaxel-based chemotherapy -- active regimen but ECOG PS 2, transfusion-dependence, and patient preference to defer chemotherapy; (b) lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto) -- PSMA PET eligible, ARSI-exposed, taxane-naive (note VISION trial required prior taxane, however this patient has significant comorbidity limiting chemotherapy and PSMA eligibility is robust); (c) clinical trial -- no currently open GU trials for which patient is eligible; (d) best supportive care alone -- discussed but patient wishes to continue active treatment.

Consensus recommendation: Proceed with lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto) radioligand therapy. Acknowledge deviation from VISION trial eligibility (taxane-naive), but team consensus that benefit/risk favors this approach given high PSMA expression, declining performance status, transfusion-dependent anemia precluding safe chemotherapy delivery, and patient values.

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with mCRPC, now with confirmed visceral (hepatic) and multi-site osseous progression following olaparib for BRCA2-mutated disease. Prior therapy line summary: ADT + abiraterone/prednisone --> definitive RT --> enzalutamide --> olaparib (all discontinued due to progression). Significant cumulative toxicity burden including transfusion-dependent anemia (PARP inhibitor-related myelosuppression). ECOG PS 2. PSMA PET/CT demonstrates robust PSMA expression across all lesions.

1. **Next-line therapy -- lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (177Lu-PSMA-617, Pluvicto):**
   - Initiated referral to nuclear medicine/theranostics team for treatment planning and scheduling
   - Standard dosing: 7.4 GBq IV every 6 weeks x 6 cycles planned (per VISION protocol)
   - Leuprolide (ADT) to be continued throughout
   - Denosumab (Xgeva) to be continued for bone protection; ensure dental clearance prior to each denosumab cycle
   - Pre-treatment labs including CBC, CMP, PSA, testosterone confirmed today
   - Radiation safety counseling to be provided by nuclear medicine team
   - Renal function monitoring (eGFR 68 mL/min -- adequate for treatment)
   - Salivary gland protection measures (lemon drops/candy) will be recommended by theranostics team

2. **Anemia management:**
   - Olaparib discontinued; expect gradual marrow recovery over weeks to months
   - Continue IV iron supplementation (ferric carboxymaltose) per hematology protocol
   - Monitor Hgb every 2-3 weeks; transfuse for symptomatic anemia or Hgb < 8 g/dL
   - Hold lutetium-177 PSMA-617 if Hgb < 8 g/dL at time of planned infusion (coordinate with nuclear medicine)
   - Hematology to remain co-managing

3. **Bone health / skeletal protection:**
   - Denosumab 120 mg SC monthly: continue
   - Ensure calcium + vitamin D supplementation continued
   - Monitor for jaw osteonecrosis (dental exam to be confirmed current)
   - MRI spine was reassuring for no cord compromise at this time; re-image if new neurological symptoms

4. **Symptom management / palliative care:**
   - Palliative care formally co-managing; appointment confirmed for this week
   - Right upper quadrant discomfort: likely from hepatic lesion; consider low-dose dexamethasone short course for symptom relief if worsening, pending discussion with palliative care
   - Nutritional consult placed given 8 lb weight loss and albumin 3.2 g/dL; high-calorie oral supplements recommended
   - Fatigue counseling: anticipate some improvement in energy as anemia recovers off olaparib
   - Pain management: continue as-needed NSAIDs/acetaminophen; escalate to low-dose opioids if inadequate (coordinate with palliative care)

5. **Goals of care:**
   - Extensive discussion held today with patient and wife. Goals articulated as: maximize quality of life, maintain functional independence, disease control -- patient understands treatment intent is palliative at this stage, not curative. He has an advance directive on file. He declines hospice at this time, preferring to continue active treatment. He was receptive to the discussion and expressed understanding.
   - POLST form reviewed and updated

6. **Diabetes and hypertension:**
   - Metformin and lisinopril continued at current doses
   - Monitor renal function closely given lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (renal excretion)

7. **Follow-up:**
   - Nuclear medicine / theranostics consultation within 2 weeks for 177Lu-PSMA-617 planning
   - Labs (CBC, CMP, PSA, LFTs) in 3 weeks
   - Return to medical oncology clinic 6 weeks after first 177Lu-PSMA-617 infusion for response assessment and toxicity review
   - Patient instructed to call with worsening fatigue, new or worsening pain, neurological symptoms, fever, or jaundice

Time spent with patient and family: 65 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

-- Dictated and electronically signed by attending GU Medical Oncologist
